The Future of Energy with Jane Smith

Jane Smith, Energy Economist

February 20, 2024
HOST: Welcome to Cheeky Pint. Today we have Jane Smith who has spent twenty years studying energy transitions. Jane, let's start with the basics. Why is the energy transition so hard? JANE: The fundamental challenge is that energy is the foundation of everything. Every sector of the economy runs on it. So transitioning away from fossil fuels requires simultaneous transformation of electricity generation, transportation, heating, industrial processes, and agriculture. Each of these sectors has its own inertia and its own political economy and they all need to change at roughly the same time. HOST: And yet we're seeing solar costs fall dramatically. Isn't that enough to drive the transition on its own? JANE: Solar cost reduction is genuinely remarkable. We've seen a ninety percent cost decline in the last decade. But cost is only one part of the equation. The other parts are intermittency, grid infrastructure, storage, and the political economy of stranded assets. A solar panel that only generates power when the sun is shining needs to be paired with storage or backup generation to be truly useful. HOST: Tell me about stranded assets because I think this is underappreciated. JANE: If we transition to clean energy rapidly it means that enormous amounts of capital invested in fossil fuel infrastructure becomes worthless before it's fully depreciated. Coal plants, oil pipelines, refineries, gas stations. The owners of these assets have massive incentives to slow the transition. And many of these assets are held by pension funds and ordinary investors which creates a broad political constituency for slowing down. HOST: What role does nuclear power play in your thinking about the energy transition? JANE: Nuclear is genuinely complicated. On the one hand it produces zero carbon electricity reliably regardless of weather conditions. On the other hand it's expensive, slow to build, and faces massive public opposition in many countries after Fukushima and Chernobyl. I think advanced nuclear technologies like small modular reactors could be important in the long run but I'm skeptical they'll be a major factor in the next decade. HOST: What about green hydrogen? I hear about it constantly but am not sure what to make of it. JANE: Green hydrogen is real and important but often misunderstood. The basic idea is that you use surplus renewable electricity to split water into hydrogen and oxygen through electrolysis. That hydrogen can then be used as a fuel or feedstock in industries that are hard to electrify directly like steel production, shipping, and fertilizer manufacturing. The problem is efficiency losses at every step of the chain. HOST: What's your best case scenario for 2050? JANE: Best case is that we achieve about eighty percent clean electricity globally, that electric vehicles dominate new vehicle sales, and that we've made significant progress on industrial decarbonization through green hydrogen and carbon capture. In this scenario we're on track to limit warming to around one point five degrees Celsius. HOST: And worst case? JANE: Worst case is that we're still heavily dependent on fossil fuels in 2050 because we failed to build out the necessary infrastructure fast enough and because political resistance proved stronger than technological progress. In this scenario we're looking at two to three degrees of warming with severe consequences for ecosystems, agriculture, and coastal populations. HOST: Thank you Jane for this fascinating conversation about one of the defining challenges of our time. JANE: My pleasure. These are the most important questions of our time and I'm glad more people are engaging seriously with the complexity of the transition rather than treating it as either impossible or trivially easy.
